Persistent knowledge and skills live in parameters as circuits, attention heads routing or moving information and MLPs computing or storing, composed through the shared residual stream; step-by-step reasoning externalizes computation into the context window as scratch memory when a problem needs more sequential steps than the model has layers.

Depth, the number of layers, bounds how many sequential computation steps a model can chain in a single forward pass. When a problem needs more steps than that, writing intermediate results into the context and reading them back on a later step, chain-of-thought, lets the model reuse its fixed per-layer computation repeatedly, trading context length for depth. Circuits generalize, rather than memorize, only when training data is too systematically varied for memorization to be cheaper than learning the general computation.

This concept explains two things bearing on the project. First, why forced instruction-following through a separate finetune phase is more fragile than co-training it into pretraining (node 30): finetuning tries to install a new usage pattern for existing circuits after they have already committed to a solution during pretraining, whereas co-training lets the same circuits form around the instruction format from the start. Second, why the corpus's early perplexity plateau at small scale reflects an information-narrow corpus rather than solely a capacity limit: a model can only build generalizing circuits, rather than memorize surface patterns, if the training distribution is varied enough that memorization is not the cheaper solution, which connects directly to the diversity-contraction problem (node 35) and motivates the world-state and reasoning goals (node 2, node 36).
